mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers subsections A1a-d on all the major crusades from the First through Louis IX's campaigns, the crusader states, and their decline.
Directly covers subsection A1c on the Byzantine Empire from 1081 to 1204 under the Comnenus dynasty and the Fourth Crusade.
Directly covers subsection A2 on the Mongolian invasions of eastern Europe and Southwest Asia and the Il-Khan and Golden Horde successor states.
Covers the Islamic states discussed in subsection B including the Seljuq Empire, Ayyubids, Mamluks, and the Ottoman rise.
Relevant to subsection B on the Islamic dynasties, Sunni orthodoxy under the Mamluks, and the religious dimensions of the Almoravid and Almohad movements.
Covers subsections A1e and A2a on Russia under Tatar rule, the rise of the Muscovite state, and the Golden Horde.
Relevant to subsection A3 on the Byzantine cultural revival under the Palaeologus dynasty and the Eastern Church.
Relevant to subsections B1a and B3a on the Seljuq Empire in Iran and the Mongol Il-Khans.
Relevant to the numerous dynastic successions discussed throughout the section, from the Seljuqs and Ayyubids to the Ottomans.
Relevant to subsection B4c-d on Almohad patronage of philosophy and the intellectual life of the Hafsids and Nasrids.
Directly relevant to the religious motivations and consequences of the crusading movement discussed in subsection A.
Relevant to subsection A1a on the disruption of pilgrimage routes by Muslims as a background cause of the First Crusade.
Relevant to the spread and evolution of Islam across North Africa, Southwest Asia, and Anatolia discussed in subsection B.
